/notes/encrypted-nixos.title: Install encrypted NixOS
/notes/encrypted-nixos.content:
![encrypted nixos install](images/nixos-encrypted.jpg)

## Why NixOS

Tired of other distributions there.  Just want to try something new. Been using it a few years back. But not that deep. 

### Download 
Head over to [Downloads page](https://nixos.org/download) and get the latest iso file. I chose the minimal install iso. 

Insert a USB drive to your machine and write the NixOS image to it.

```shell
dd if=nixos.iso of=/dev/sdX bs=4M status=progress
```

Boot your machine and interrupt it by pressing the dedicated key. Boot from USB drive.

### Connect to internet

Become root by invoking `sudo -i` and connect to internet using `wpa_supplicant`.

```bash
wpa_passphrase 'your network name' 'your passphrase' > /etc/wpa_supplicant.conf
systemctl start wpa_supplicant

```

or you can use `wpa_cli` to connect.


### Partitioning

Suppose your drive is `/dev/sda`

We are going to create first boot partition unencrypted. And all other space we are going to allocate to `LUKS volume` so 2 partitions.

```bash
gdisk /dev/sda


GPT fdisk (gdisk) version 1.0.1

  Partition table scan:
  MBR: protective
  BSD: not present
  APM: not present
  GPT: present

Found valid GPT with protective MBR; using GPT.

Command (? for help): o
This option deletes all partitions and creates a new protective MBR.
Proceed? (Y/N): Y

Command (? for help): n
Partition number (1-128, default 1): 
First sector (34-242187466, default = 2048) or {+-}size{KMGTP}: 
Last sector (2048-242187466, default = 242187466) or {+-}size{KMGTP}:
+512M
Current type is 'Linux filesystem'
Hex code or GUID (L to show codes, Enter = 8300): EF00
Changed type of partition to 'EFI System'

Command (? for help): n
Partition number (2-128, default 2): 
First sector (34-242187466, default = 1050624) or {+-}size{KMGTP}: 
Last sector (1050624-242187466, default = 242187466) or {+-}size{KMGTP}: 
Current type is 'Linux filesystem'
Hex code or GUID (L to show codes, Enter = 8300): 8309
Changed type of partition to 'Linux LUKS'

Command (? for help): p
Disk /dev/sda: 242187500 sectors, 115.5 GiB
Logical sector size: 512 bytes
Disk identifier (GUID): 569FFB67-6969-49E4-B999-15A681987564
Partition table holds up to 128 entries
First usable sector is 34, last usable sector is 242187466
Partitions will be aligned on 2048-sector boundaries
Total free space is 2014 sectors (1007.0 KiB)

Number  Start (sector)    End (sector)  Size       Code  Name
   1            2048         1050623   512.0 MiB   EF00  EFI System
   2         1050624       242187466   115.0 GiB   8309  Linux LUKS

command (? for help): w


```

Type o and then Y.

Type n to create a new partition. Select 1 to create a very first partition for EFI. Hit enter and then type +512M to create a 512 Mbs partition. Enter ef00 as partition type.

Again type n to create a new partition. Select 2. Hit enter and again enter to give it rest of the storage if you don’t wanna install some other operating system. Hex code for LUKS is 8309 but you can go without it. By default it will be 8300 (linux filesystem).

Type p to check if everything is fine. And once satisfied, type w and hit enter to save the work.


### Create encrypted container

Now create a LUKS container.
```bash
cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sda2
```

It will ask you to confirm. Type YES in all uppercase. Then choose a good passphrase. Write it if you can’t memorize it. Once forgotten, you can’t recover it.

Now open the new container.

```shell
cryptsetup open /dev/sda2 cryptlvm

```
Create a physical volume in this LUKS container.

```bash
pvcreate /dev/mapper/cryptlvm

```

Create a volume group. I’ll name it nix.

```bash
vgcreate nix /dev/mapper/cryptlvm
```

Create logical volumes on volume group. I’m creating three, one for encrypted swap and another for home and last one for root.

```
lvcreate -L 8G nix -n swap
lvcreate -L 25G nix -n root
lvcreate -l +100%FREE nix -n home

```

### Formating Drive

Next we are going to create filesystems on it.

First partition will have FAT filesystem and we will add a label `nixos-boot` to it. 

```shell
mkfs.fat -F 32 -n nix-boot /dev/sda1
```

Next we will create `ext4` filesystems for `root` and `home`. And add `swap`.

```shell
mkfs.ext4 /dev/mapper/nix-root -L nix-root
mkfs.ext4 /dev/mapper/nix-home -L nix-home
mkswap /dev/mapper/nix-swap -L nix-swap

```

### Mount the partitions

Next we will mount the partitions. Order is important. First of all, we will mount the root, then create directories and mount the other filesystems.


```bash
mount /dev/disk/by-label/nix-root /mnt
mkdir -p /mnt/home
mkdir -p /mnt/boot
mount /dev/disk/by-label/nix-home /mnt/home
mount /dev/disk/by-label/nix-boot /mnt/boot
swapon /dev/disk/by-label/nix-swap
```

### Generate NixOS  configuration 

Next we have to generate NixOS configuration file. All OS is based on this configuration file.

```bash
nixos-generate-config --root /mnt

```

Let's edit this file to have system like how we want. If you're installing using minimal iso file, you should have `nano` only. In that case you can install `vim` .

```bash

# In case you don't have vim

nix-env -f '<nixpkgs>' -iA vim
```
And edit the configuration file.
```
vi /mnt/etc/nixos/configuration.nix
```

Add a user and don't forget to enable `systemd-boot` else your system won't boot.

```bash
# User management

    users = {
        users.ak = {
            isNormalUser = true;
            shell = pkgs.zsh;
            extraGroups = [
            "wheel" "ak" "docker" "video" "audio"
            ];
        }
    }

```
Replace `ak` with your desired username. Don't forget to set a password.

### Install

Let's install the OS now.

```bash
nixos-install
```
It witll install the OS based on your configuration. At the last step, it will ask you the root password. Just set the root password and reboot.

```bash

setting root password...
New password: ***
Retype new password: ***

reboot
```


### Login 

On the first boot, login with root user, change the password, and if you previously didn't add a user, add now. 

```shell

useradd -c 'Abdullah Khabir' -m -U -G wheel,audio,video ak
passwd ak
```
Replace `ak` with your desired username, `Abdullah Khabir` with your real name. `-U` will create a group with the same name as your user, `-m` will create a home directory and with `-G` you will be added to `wheel` group to use `sudo` command, `video` group to set/change brightness.


> ✏️  Next:   [Manage Dotfiles using Home-Manager](notes/home-manager-install.md)

/notes/home-manager-install.title: Home Manager Installation
/notes/home-manager-install.content:
## What's Home Manager

Home-Manager is a nix way to manage dotfiles.

### How to install

Home-Manager can be installed on almost any linux distribution. But I'm covering only NixOS here.

```shell
nix-channel --add https://github.com/nix-community/home-manager/archive/release-22.05.tar.gz home-manager
```

Replace `release-22.05` with the release you're using.

And update channels.

```shell
nix-channel --update
```

Install home-manager.

```shell
nix-shell '<home-manager>' -A install
```

### Back up old dots

If you already have some dots, home-manager will warn you. Back them up and re-run the last command.

### Edit home-manager file

home-manager will create a file `~/.config/nixpkgs/home.nix`. You can edit it by hand to suit your needs. Or by invoking:

```shell
home-manager edit
```
